analyst_observations: The skill achieves 100% pass rate across all 3 test cases vs 37.8% baseline — a +62.2pp improvement.

Scan-triage shows the largest structural gap: baseline produces ad-hoc analysis (useful but unstructured) while the skill produces the exact queue format needed for the spaced-repetition workflow. Baseline also fails to exclude no-review tagged notes.

Full-mode skill enforces pedagogically sound structure: Feynman check before adversarial questions, constrained question counts (5+3), and English correction. Baseline dumps 16+10 questions — thorough but overwhelming for a single review session.
Quick-mode skill adds structured question typing (definition/application/cross-link) that baseline lacks. Both produce 3 questions, but the skill ensures topic coverage diversity.

Scan-triage baseline took 490s (3.2x longer than with-skill at 155s) because it read more note content and produced verbose per-note listings. The skill's focused frontmatter-only scan is more efficient.

The baseline produces higher-quality physics analysis in some areas (the full-mode C- grade breakdown is excellent), suggesting the raw capability is there — the skill's value is in structuring and constraining this capability into a repeatable workflow.
Non-discriminating assertion: 'date_aware_categorization' passed for both configurations in scan-triage. Claude naturally understands dates well.
Non-discriminating assertion: 'all_presented_at_once' and 'concise_format' both pass for baseline quick-mode. These are easy for Claude without a skill.
